rulings.Case Title: PERSOLVE, LLC A LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY, DBA VS. CASSANDRA Y PIERCE AKA CASSANDRA V PIERCE, AN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: HEARING ON CLAIM OF EXEMPTION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 11, PLAINTIFF PERSOLVE, LLC A LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY, DBA HEARING ON CLAIM OF EXEMPTION: Hearing required. Defendant debtor apparently claims that the funds that the Sheriff are holding are exempt social security payments. (See 42 USCA 407; CCP sec. 704.080(c); Kilker v. Stillman (2015) 233 Cal.App.4th 320.) A hearing is required and defendant shall provide a copy of her claim for the court to review. Defendant shall also present any proof that she has that the funds are exempt social security payments or proof demonstrating another applicable exemption. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: DANIEL FRANKLIN VS. MASTEK INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Daniel Franklin'S Motion To Quash Subpoena Of Out-Of-State Witness (Per Ex Parte Order Filed 5/6/2021)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 7, Plaintiff Daniel Franklin's Motion To Quash Subpoena Of Out-Of-State Witness (Per Ex Parte Order Filed 5/6/2021). Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. The Presiding Judge has not issued an order clarifying previous order. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPT 301 @ 9:00AM), or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to williamclynn@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN RUSSELL VS. TARA WALSH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Tara Walsh'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling):
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 1, (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry.) Defendant Tara Walsh'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling): Battery is "not limited to direct body-to-body contact" and "one is liable for battery if one throws a substance, such as water, upon the other ." (Mount Vernon Fire Insurance Corp. v. Oxnard Hospitality Enterprise, Inc. (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 876, 881.) "The touch can be done indirectly by causing an object to touch the other person, andthe slightest touching can constitute a battery." (People v. Dealba (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 1142, 1150.) The described conduct is "extreme and outrageous." (Hailey v. California Physicians' Service (2007) 158 Cal.App.4th 452, 473-474.) It is irrelevant that on prior occasions plaintiff may have agreed to ingest drugs. Slipping plaintiff drugs without his consent is a completely different animal. The domestic abuse claim is well-pleaded given the broad definition of "abuse." (Pugliese v. Superior Court (2007) 146 Cal.App.4th 1444, 1448-1449.) The drug-dealing claim is also well-pleaded as it applies to prescription drugs. (See Whittemore v. Owens Healthcare-Retail Pharmacy, Inc. (2010) 185 Cal.App.4th 1194, 1196.) Walsh notes that plaintiff previously alleged that the drugging/s were negligent. That prior allegation, which the New York Court rejected with regard to the single incident in New York, does not defeat plaintiff's claims. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN RUSSELL VS. TARA WALSH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 1, (Part 1 of 2 for tentative ruling) DEFENDANT TARA WALSH's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings: Defendant Tara Walsh's common law motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied. The court rejects Walsh's argument that claim preclusion bars this action. (See DKN Holdings LLC v. Faerber (2015) 61 Cal.4th 813, 824 [noting preference for use of the phrase "claim preclusion" over "res judicata"].) First, Walsh fails to demonstrate that the New York action involved the "same transaction or series of transactions" for purposes of claim preclusion. (Silberstein, Awad & Miklos, P.C. v. Spencer, Maston & McCarthy, LLP (841 N.Y.S. 2d 623, 624.) Walsh cites no law that allows a New York court to adjudicate torts that occurred in California. Moreover, the New York court itself stated that its action was limited to the alleged New York drugging. (Walsh's RJN, Ex. D ["In this action to recover damages for personal injuries from defendant's alleged surreptitious administration of drugs to plaintiff on January 30, 2018, in the city, state, and county of New York" and noting that the California action "differs in nature from the instant claims"].) Second, New York presently does not consider a dismissal based on the statute of limitations as a determination "on the merits" to invoke claim preclusion. (See Cloverleaf Realty of N.Y., Inc. v. Town of Wawayanda, 572 F.3d 93, 95-96 (2009) ["New York law does not depart from the traditional rule" and "a dismissal of a claim solely for lack of timeliness in a New York state court does not preclude the same claim from being brought in another jurisdiction with a longer statute of limitations." (Id. at 96. [citing Tanges v. Heidelberg N. Am., Inc., 93 N.Y.2d 48, 55 (1999)].) Third, while Walsh's moving papers refer to New York law, most of the analysis in her moving papers improperly relies on California law to resolve whether claim preclusion applies. (See Walsh's moving points and authorities, 3:20-4:19.) "Under California law, both the validity and effect of a foreign judgment are governed by the laws of the state in which it is rendered. (Krofcheck v. Ensign Co. (1980) 112 Cal.App.3d 558, 169 Cal.Rptr. 516.)" (Brinker v. Superior Court (1991) 235 Cal.App.3d 1296, 1300; see also Gagnon Co. v. Nevada Desert Inn (1955) 45 Cal.2d 448, 453 [Nevada law controlled whether Nevada judgment barred subsequent action in California based on claim preclusion]; Hawkins v. SunTrust Bank (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1387, 1391-1392 [applying South Carolina law to determine whether South Carolina judgment had preclusive effect in subsequent California action].) Walsh's moving papers do not sufficiently brief the elements of claim preclusion under New York law. Plaintiff's allegations of surreptitious druggings support his claims for battery, intentional infliction of emotional distress, domestic violence (Civil Code sec. 1708.6), and drug dealer liability (Health & Safety Code sec. 11700 et seq.) (tentative ruling continued, see next entry) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: TODD J. FERRARIS VS. UNITED PARCEL SERVICE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Request For Judicial Notice Declaration Of Steven L Derby And Proposed Order Granting Judgment For Plaintiff With Court Reporter Fee
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 4, PLAINTIFF TODD FERRARIS' Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings: Plaintiff's motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied. This motion is analogous to a demurrer to an answer. The Court therefore takes defendant's allegations as true and does not resolve contested factual issues. (See Allstate Ins. Co. v. Kim W. (1984) 160 Cal.App.3d 326, 330-331; Sebago, Inc. v. City of Alameda (1989) 211 Cal.App.3d 1372, 1379-1380.) The parties apparently agree that defense 18 [legitimate reason for adverse employment action] is not a "defense." Nevertheless, whether defendant had a legitimate non-invidious reason for its conduct is an issue that will be adjudicated in this case. The court sees no point in granting the motion as to this issue. The court denies judgment on the pleadings as to the other adduced defenses (19, 21, 22, and 23) because they raise contested factual issues. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: BERT LEE VS. SAN FRANCISCO FEDERAL CREDIT UNION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 5, DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO FEDERAL CREDIT UNION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION: Defendant San Francisco Federal Credit Union moves for summary adjudication on two of the complaint's nine counts: retaliation in violation of Labor Code 1102.5 (count 1) and race discrimination (count 6). Plaintiff's opposition does not mention - and thus abandons - count 6, so summary adjudication is granted on it. In count 1, the complaint alleges: "Defendant retaliated against Plaintiff for his reports about suspected money laundering involving an elderly patron" "It is to the allegations in the complaint to which the summary judgment motion must respond." (Laabs v. City of Victorville (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 1242, 1258.) However, defendant's memorandum does not respond to the allegations in the complaint regarding retaliation for reporting "money laundering." Neither the term "money laundering" nor its concept appears in defendant's memo save in one quotation from the complaint. Thus, summary adjudication is denied on count 1. (Id.) Even were the complaint's "money laundering" allegations to be impermissibly recast as "elder financial abuse" allegations, triable issues of material fact would exist, and thus summary adjudication would still be denied on count 1. Plaintiff met his minimal prima facie burden by showing (1) his protected activity of whistleblowing about a caretaker's attempts to take hundreds of thousands from an elder's account with defendant, (2) plaintiff's firing and (3) the temporal proximity between whistleblowing and firing. (Manavian v. Department of Justice (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 1127, 1141; AMF 46, 51, 53, 56, 57.) Defendant adduces a legitimate, non-retaliatory reason for the firing: plaintiff's purported failure to improve his job performance. (UMF 16.) However, plaintiff responds with sufficient evidence of pretext: managers' failure to notify authorities of the alleged financial abuse, differing stories on whether the elder suffered dementia and contradictions in a manager's sworn testimony. (AMF 81-87, 89, 91-95, 97-98, 102, 113, 119, 121, 125-127.) As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: SAMIRA KHARRUBI VS. COLUMBUS RESIDENCE, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment By Defendant Jb Maintenance Against Plaintiffs Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 10, DEFENDANT JB MAINTENANCE's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Against Plaintiff's Complaint: Defendant JB Maintenance's summary judgment motion is granted. Plaintiff sued after slipping and falling on water at an apartment building. Defendant Charybdis, LLC leases the property to defendant Columbus Residence LLC, which contracted with JB for cleaning services. The contract calls for JB to indemnify Columbus for JB's negligence, but no party filed a cross-claim. JB moved for summary judgment on plaintiff's complaint. Plaintiff filed a non-opposition; she says facts learned in discovery make her claim against JB unviable. However, Columbus and Charybdis filed a "partial opposition" to JB's summary judgment motion. Whether they had standing to do so was not addressed in the papers, so I asked for, and received, supplemental briefing. Based on that briefing, no citable California state court decision directly addresses opposition by a defendant with no cross-claim to another defendant's summary judgment motion. Atlas Construction Supply, Inc. v. Swinerton Builders (2021) 274 Cal.Rptr. 414 was recently depublished. Columbus Line, Inc. v. Gray Line Sight-Seeing Companies Associated, Inc. (1981) 120 Cal.App.3d 622 had cross-claims. However, a decision by a California federal court holds that a co-defendant without a cross-claim lacks standing to oppose a summary judgment motion. (Eckert v. City of Sacramento (2009) 2009 WL 3211278; see also Fraioli v. Lemcke (D.R.I. 2004) 328 F.Supp.2d 250, 263; Blonder v. Casco Inn Residential Care, Inc. (D. Me. 2000) WL 761895.) I am persuaded by the logic of these decisions. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MELINDA MIYAGISHIMA VS. AVISON YOUNG - NORTHERN CALIFORNIA, LTD. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Avison Young Northern California, Ltd.S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 7, (Part 1 of 2 for tentative ruling) DEFENDANT AVISON YOUNG - NORTHERN CALIFORNIA, LTD.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT: Defendant Avision Young's summary judgment motion is denied in its entirety. Summary adjudication is denied as to plaintiff's first cause of action for retaliation. Triable issues of fact exist as to whether plaintiff's complaint to human resources constitutes "protected activity" under Labor Code section 1102.5(b). Defendant argues plaintiff did not engage in protected activity under the Whistleblower Statute because plaintiff's report concerned "internal personnel matters" only. (See Moore Decl., Ex. B, Plaintiff's Depo. 62:2-19; see also Patten v. Grant Joint Union High School Dist. (2005) 134 Cal.App.4th 1378, 1385 [involved an exclusive internal personnel disclosure].) However, plaintiff presents evidence that she reasonably believed Mr. Slonek was intoxicated numerous times at work and was "very concerned if he was going to kill someone, if not himself, on the road." (Index of Exs. ISO Opp., Miyagishima Depo. 46:12-47:7, 48:6-49:25.) Additionally, triable issues of fact exist as to whether there is a causal link between plaintiff's 2018 report concerning Mr. Slonek's alcohol consumption and plaintiff's layoff in 2020. (See Hawkins v. City of Los Angeles (2019) 40 Cal.App.5th 384, 394 [causal connection could be established so long as the employer engaged in a pattern of conduct consistent with retaliatory intent].) Plaintiff presents evidence that once she reported Mr. Slonek, he began treating her differently, in a negative way. (Index of Exs. ISO Opp., Miyagashima Depo. 155:13-156:1; see also Miyagashima Decl. paras. 3, 9, 12.) Defendant states it laid off certain employees due to Covid-19 (UMF 15-16), however, triable issues of fact regarding pretext exists and must be decided by the jury. Plaintiff's evidence calls into question defendant's five stated reasons for plaintiff's layoff. (See UMF 24.) For example, plaintiff presents evidence that she was not "extremely slow" and that colleagues considered her to be competent. (Index of Exs. ISO Opp., Simpson Decl. 18:15-23:15.) Summary adjudication is denied as to plaintiff's second cause of action for age-based discrimination. The court must consider "all of the evidence set forth in the papersand all reasonable inferences reasonably deducible from the evidence." (See Reid v. Google, Inc. (2010) 50 Cal.4th 512, 541.) Mr. Slonek's comment about plaintiff's age in 2016, plus the testimony of Akina, Soria, and Simpson call into question the truth of defendant's five stated reasons for laying off plaintiff. (See UMF 24; see Moore Decl., Ex. B, Pls. Depo. 148:19-149:3 ["I think he meant that I was getting stale and old, particularly in commercial real estate."].) Triable issues of fact exist as to pretext. Plaintiff's discrimination and retaliation claims survive; therefore, summary adjudication is denied as to plaintiff's failure to prevent discrimination and wrongful termination claims. Likewise, plaintiff's claim of punitive damages must also go to the jury because Mr. Slonek's actions could be found malicious or oppressive. (tentative ruling continued, see next entry) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL TONG ET AL VS. STEPHEN F. GRUEL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Documents And Further Responses To Defendants First Set Of Requests For Production Of Documents And First Set Of Special Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 6, DEFENDANTS STEPHEN GRUEL AND LAW OFFICES OF STEPHEN F. GRUEL's Motion To Compel Documents And Further Responses To Defendants First Set Of Requests For Production Of Documents And First Set Of Special Interrogatories. Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPT 301 @ 9:00AM), or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to williamclynn@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN RUSSELL VS. STEPHEN WALSH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion Of Quash
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, May 21, 2021, Line 8, DEFENDANT STEPHEN WALSH's Motion Of Quash: Defendant Stephen Walsh's motion to quash is granted. "When a nonresident defendant challenges personal jurisdiction the burden shifts to the plaintiff to demonstrate by a preponderance of the evidence that all necessary jurisdictional criteria are met. [citation] This burden must be met by competent evidence in affidavits and authenticated documentary evidence." (Ziller Electronics Lab GmbH v. Superior Court (1988) 206 Cal.App.3d 1222, 1232-1233; see also BBA Aviation PLC v. Superior Court (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 421, 428-429 ["The plaintiff must do more than merely allege jurisdictional facts; plaintiff must provide affidavits and other authenticated documents demonstrating competent evidence of jurisdictional facts."].) "It is correct that a motion to quash for lack of personal jurisdiction does not implicate the merits of the complaint, but the plaintiff, in opposing the defendant's motion to quash, must present evidence to justify a finding that the requisite jurisdictional minimum contacts exist." (Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 540; see also DVI, Inc. v. Superior Court (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 1080, 1094.) Plaintiff claims that Stephen Walsh, a New York resident, is subject to specific jurisdiction in California based on his fraudulent e-mail and text communications. "Specific jurisdiction exists if: (1) the defendant has purposefully availed itself of forum benefits with respect to the matter in controversy; (2) the controversy is related to or arises out of the defendant's contacts with the forum; and (3) the assertion of jurisdiction would comport with fair play and substantial justice." (Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 536.) Here, Stephen Walsh was not purposefully availing himself of forum benefits. His vague communications to plaintiff do not demonstrate any sort of fraudulent conspiracy to dupe plaintiff. In addition, those communications show that plaintiff was reaching out to Mr. Walsh. Mr. Walsh is not strictly liable for his adult daughter's decision to leave California and allegedly violate a temporary restraining order. Plaintiff therefore fails to demonstrate sufficient minimum contacts to warrant specific jurisdiction. Plaintiff's request for jurisdictional discovery is denied. As explained, the adduced communications are too vague to support jurisdiction and plaintiff fails to explain what other information he hopes to unearth that will establish jurisdiction. (See In re Automobile Antitrust Cases I and II (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 100, 127 [court did not abuse discretion in denying jurisdictional discovery].) As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
